text: And the king rose before it was light, and made his troops march on fiercely towards the way of Bethzacharam: and the armies made themselves ready for the battle, and they sounded the trumpets:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they shewed the elephants the blood of grapes, and mulberries, to provoke them to fight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they distributed the beasts by the legions: and there stood by every elephant a thousand men in coats of mail, and with helmets of brass on their heads: and five hundred horsemen set in order were chosen for every beast.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These before the time wheresoever the beast was they were there: and whithersoever it went, they went, and they departed not from it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And upon the beast, there were strong wooden towers which covered every one of them: and engines upon them, and upon every one thirty-two valiant men, who fought from above: and an Indian to rule the beast.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the rest of the horsemen he placed on this side and on that side, at the two wings, with trumpets to stir up the army, and to hasten them forward that stood thick together in the legions thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when the sun shone upon the shields of gold, and of brass, the mountains glittered therewith, and they shone like lamps of fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And part of the king’s army was distinguished by the high mountains, and the other part by the low places: and they marched on warily and orderly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the inhabitants of the land were moved at the noise of their multitude, and the marching of the company, and the rattling of the armour, for the army was exceeding great and strong.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Judas and his army drew near for battle: and there fell of the king’s army six hundred men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Eleazar, the son of Saura, saw one of the beasts harnessed with the king’s harness: and it was higher than the other beasts; and it seemed to him that the king was on it:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he exposed himself to deliver his people, and to get himself an everlasting name.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he ran up to it boldly in the midst of the legion, killing on the right hand, and on the left, and they fell by him on this side and that side.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he went between the feet of the elephant, and put himself under it: and slew it, and it fell to the ground upon him, and he died there.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then they seeing the strength of the king and the fierceness of his army, turned away from them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the king’s army went up against them to Jerusalem: and the king’s army pitched their tents against Judea and Mount Sion.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he made peace with them that were in Bethsura: and they came forth out of the city, because they had no victuals, being shut up there, for it was the year of rest to the land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king took Bethsura: and he placed there a garrison to keep it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he turned his army against the sanctuary for many days: and he set up there battering slings, and engines, and instruments to cast fire, and engines to cast stones and javelins, and pieces to shoot arrows, and slings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they also made engines against their engines, and they fought for many days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But there were no victuals in the city, because it was the seventh year: and such as had stayed in Judea of them that came from among the nations, had eaten the residue of all that which had been stored up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there remained in the holy places but a few, for the famine had prevailed over them: and they were dispersed every man to his own place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Lysias heard that Philip; whom king Antiochus while he lived had appointed to bring up his son, Antiochus, and to reign,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Was returned from Persia, and Media, with the army that went with him and that he sought to take upon him the affairs of the kingdom:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore he made haste to go, and say to the king and to the captains of the army: We decay daily, and our provision of victuals is small, and the place that we lay siege to is strong, and it lieth upon us to take order for the affairs of the kingdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, therefore, let us come to an agreement with these men, and make peace with them and with all their nation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And let us covenant with them, that they may live according to their own laws, as before. For because of our despising their laws, they have been provoked, and have done all these things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the proposal was acceptable in the sight of the king, and of the princes: and he sent to them to make peace: and they accepted of it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king and the princes swore to them: and they came out of the strong hold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king entered into Mount Sion, and saw the strength of the place: and he quickly broke the oath that he had taken, and gave commandment to throw down the wall round about.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he departed in haste and returned to Antioch, where he found Philip master of the city: and he fought against him, and took the city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the hundred and fifty-first year, Demetrius, the son of Seleucus, departed from the city of Rome, and came up with few men into a city of the sea coast, and reigned there.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it came to pass as he entered into the house of the kingdom of his fathers, that the army seized upon Antiochus, and Lysias, to bring them unto him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he knew it, he said: Let me not see their face.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the army slew them. And Demetrius sat upon the throne of his kingdom:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there came to him the wicked and ungodly men of Israel: and Alcimus was at the head of them, who desired to be made high priest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they accused the people to the king, saying: Judas and his brethren have destroyed all thy friends, and he hath driven us out of our land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, therefore, send some men whom thou trustest, and let him go, and see all the havoc he hath made amongst us, and in the king’s lands: and let him punish all his friends and their helpers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king chose Bacchides, one of his friends, that ruled beyond the great river in the kingdom, and was faithful to the king: and he sent him,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To see the havoc that Judas had made: and the wicked Alcimus he made high priest, and commanded him to take revenge upon the children of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they arose, and came with a great army into the land of Juda: and they sent messengers, and spoke to Judas and his brethren with peaceable words, deceitfully.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they gave no heed to their words: for they saw that they were come with a great army.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then there assembled to Alcimus and Bacchides a company of the scribes, to require things that are just:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And first the Assideans, that were among the children of Israel, and they sought peace of them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they said: One that is a priest of the seed of Aaron is come, he will not deceive us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he spoke to them peaceably: and he swore to them, saying: We will do you no harm, nor your friends.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they believed him. And he took threescore of them, and slew them in one day, according to the word that is written:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The flesh of thy saints, and the blood of them they have shed round about Jerusalem, and there was none to bury them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then fear and trembling fell upon all the people: for they said: There is no truth, nor justice among them: for they have broken the covenant, and the oath which they made.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Bacchides removed the camp from Jerusalem, and pitched in Bethzecha: and he sent, and took many of them that were fled away from him, and some of the people he killed, and threw them into a great pit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then he committed the country to Alcimus, and left with him troops to help him. So Bacchides went away to the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Alcimus did what he could to maintain his chief priesthood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they that disturbed the people resorted to him, and they got the land of Juda into their power, and did much hurt in Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Judas saw all the evils that Alcimus, and they that were with him, did to the children of Israel, much more than the Gentiles.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he went out into all the coasts of Judea round about, and took vengeance upon the men that had revolted, and they ceased to go forth any more into the country.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Alcimus saw that Judas and they that were with him, prevailed: and he knew that he could not stand against them, and he went back to the king, and accused them of many crimes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king sent Nicanor, one of his principal lords, who was a great enemy to Israel: and he commanded him to destroy the people.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Nicanor came to Jerusalem with a great army, and he sent to Judas and to his brethren deceitfully, with friendly words,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Saying: Let there be no fighting between me and you: I will come with a few men, to see your faces with peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he came to Judas, and they saluted one another peaceably: and the enemies were prepared to take away Judas by force.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the thing was known to Judas that he was come to him with deceit: and he was much afraid of him, and would not see his face any more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Nicanor knew that his counsel was discovered: and he went out to fight against Judas, near Capharsalama.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there fell of Nicanor’s army almost five thousand men, and they fled into the city of David.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after this Nicanor went up into mount Sion: and some of the priests and the people came out to salute him peaceably, and to shew him the holocausts that were offered for the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he mocked and despised them, and abused them: and he spoke proudly,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And swore in anger, saying: Unless Judas and his army be delivered into my hands, as soon as ever I return in peace, I will burn this house. And he went out in a great rage.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the priests went in, and stood before the face of the altar and the temple: and weeping, they said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou, O Lord, hast chosen this house for thy name to be called upon therein, that it might be a house of prayer and supplication for thy people.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be avenged of this man, and his army, and let them fall by the sword: remember their blasphemies, and suffer them not to continue any longer.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Nicanor went out from Jerusalem, and encamped near to Bethoron: and an army of Syria joined him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Judas pitched in Adarsa with three thousand men: and Judas prayed, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O Lord, when they that were sent by king Sennacherib blasphemed thee, an angel went out, and slew of them a hundred and eighty-five thousand:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Even so destroy this army in our sight today and let the rest know that he hath spoken ill against thy sanctuary: and judge thou him according to his wickedness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the armies joined battle on the thirteenth day of the month, Adar: and the army of Nicanor was defeated, and he himself was first slain in the battle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when his army saw that Nicanor was slain they threw away their weapons, and fled:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they pursued after them one day’s journey from Adazer, even till ye come to Gazara, and they sounded the trumpets after them with signals.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they went forth out of all the towns of Judea round about, and they pushed them with the horns, and they turned again to them, and they were all slain with the sword, and there was not left of them so much as one.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they took the spoils of them for a booty, and they cut off Nicanor’s head, and his right hand, which he had proudly stretched out, and they brought it, and hung it up over against Jerusalem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the people rejoiced exceedingly, and they spent that day with great joy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he ordained that this day should be kept every year, being the thirteenth of the month of Adar.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the land of Juda was quiet for a short time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Judas heard of the fame of the Romans, that they are powerful and strong, and willingly agree to all things that are requested of them: and that whosoever have come to them, they have made amity with them, and that they are mighty in power.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they heard of their battles, and their noble acts which they had done in Galatia, how they had conquered them, and brought them under tribute:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And how great things they had done in the land of Spain, and that they had brought under their power the mines of silver and of gold that are there, and had gotten possession of all the place by their counsel and patience:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And had conquered places that were very far off from them, and kings that came against them from the ends of the earth, and had overthrown them with great slaughter: and the rest pay them tribute every year.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that they had defeated in battle Philip and Perses the king of the Ceteans, and the rest that had borne arms against them, and had conquered them:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And how Antiochus, the great king of Asia, who went to fight against them, having a hundred and twenty elephants, with horsemen, and chariots, and a very great army, was routed by them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And how they took him alive, and appointed to him, that both he and they that should reign after him, should pay a great tribute, and that he should give hostages, and that which was agreed upon,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the country of the Indians, and of the Medes, and of the Lydians, some of their best provinces: and those which they had taken from them, they gave to king Eumenes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that they who were in Greece, had a mind to go and to destroy them: and they had knowledge thereof,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they sent a general against them, and fought with them, and many of them were slain, and they carried away their wives, and their children captives, and spoiled them, and took possession of their land, and threw down their walls, and brought them to be their servants unto this day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the other kingdoms, and islands, that at any time had resisted them, they had destroyed and brought under their power.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But with their friends, and such as relied upon them, they kept amity, and had conquered kingdoms that were near, and that were far off: for all that heard their name, were afraid of them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That whom they had a mind to help to a kingdom, those reigned: and whom they would, they deposed from the kingdom: and they were greatly exalted.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And none of all these wore a crown, or was clothed in purple, to be magnified thereby.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that they had made themselves a senate house, and consulted daily three hundred and twenty men, that sat in council always for the people, that they might do the things that were right:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that they committed their government to one man every year, to rule over all their country, and they all obey one, and there is no envy nor jealousy amongst them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Judas chose Eupolemus, the son of John, the son of Jacob, and Jason, the son of Eleazar, and he sent them to Rome to make a league of amity and confederacy with them:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that they might take off from them the yoke of the Grecians, for they saw that they oppressed the kingdom of Israel with servitude.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they went to Rome, a very long journey, and they entered into the senate house, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Judas Machabeus, and his brethren, and the people of the Jews, have sent us to you to make alliance and peace with you, and that we may be registered your confederates and friends.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the proposal was pleasing in their sight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And this is the copy of the writing that they wrote back again, graven in tables of brass, and sent to Jerusalem, that it might be with them there for a memorial of the peace, and alliance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: GOOD SUCCESS BE TO THE ROMANS, and to the people of the Jews by sea, and by land, for ever: and far be the sword and enemy from them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if there come first any war upon the Romans, or any of their confederates, in all their dominions:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The nation of the Jews shall help them according as the time shall direct, with all their heart:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Neither shall they give them, whilst they are fighting, or furnish them with wheat, or arms, or money, or ships, as it hath seemed good to the Romans: and they shall obey their orders, without taking any thing of them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In like manner also if war shall come first upon the nation of the Jews, the Romans shall help them with all their heart, according as the time shall permit them:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there shall not be given to them that come to their aid, either wheat, or arms, or money, or ships, as it hath seemed good to the Romans: and they shall observe their orders without deceit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: According to these articles did the Romans covenant with the people of the Jews.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And, if after this, one party or the other shall have a mind to add to these articles, or take away any thing, they may do it at their pleasure: and whatsoever they shall add, or take away, shall be ratified.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover, concerning the evils that Demetrius, the king, hath done against them, we have written to him, saying: Why hast thou made thy yoke heavy upon our friends and allies, the Jews?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If, therefore, they come again to us complaining of thee, we will do them justice, and will make war against thee by sea and land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the mean time, when Demetrius heard that Nicanor and his army were fallen in battle, he sent again Bacchides and Alcimus into Judea; and the right wing of his army with them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they took the road that leadeth to Galgal, and they camped in Masaloth, which is in Arabella: and they made themselves masters of it, and slew many people.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the first month of the hundred and fifty-second year they brought the army to Jerusalem:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they arose and went to Berea, with twenty thousand men, and two thousand horsemen.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Judas had pitched his tents in Laisa, and three thousand chosen men with him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they saw the multitude of the army that they were many, and they were seized with great fear: and many withdrew themselves out of the camp, and there remained of them no more than eight hundred men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Judas saw that his army slipped away, and the battle pressed upon him, and his heart was cast down: because he had not time to gather them together, and he was discouraged.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then he said to them that remained: Let us arise, and go against our enemies, if we may be able to fight against them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they dissuaded him, saying: We shall not be able, but let us save our lives now, and return to our brethren, and then we will fight against them: for we are but few.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Judas said: God forbid we should do this thing, and flee away from them: but if our time be come, let us die manfully for our brethren, and let us not stain our glory.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the army removed out of the camp, and they stood over against them: and the horsemen were divided into two troops, and the slingers, and the archers, went before the army, and they that were in the front were all men of valour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Bacchides was in the right wing, and the legion drew near on two sides, and they sounded the trumpets:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they also that were on Judas’s side, even they also cried out, and the earth shook at the noise of the armies: and the battle was fought from morning even unto the evening.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Judas perceived that the stronger part of the army of Bacchides was on the right side, and all the stout of heart came together with him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the right wing was discomfited by them, and he pursued them even to the mount Azotus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they that were in the left wing saw that the right wing was discomfited, and they followed after Judas, and them that were with him, at their back:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the battle was hard fought, and there fell many wounded of the one side and of the other.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Judas was slain, and the rest fled away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan and Simon took Judas, their brother, and buried him in the sepulchre of their fathers, in the city of Modin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the people of Israel bewailed him with great lamentation, and they mourned for him many days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And said: How is the mighty man fallen, that saved the people of Israel!

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the rest of the words of the wars of Judas, and of the noble acts that he did, and of his greatness, are not written: for they were very many.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it came to pass, after the death of Judas, that the wicked began to put forth their heads in all the confines of Israel, and all the workers of iniquity rose up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In those days there was a very great famine, and they and all their country yielded to Bacchides.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Bacchides chose the wicked men, and made them lords of the country:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they sought out, and made diligent search after the friends of Judas, and brought them to Bacchides, and he took vengeance of them, and abused them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there was a great tribulation in Israel, such as was not since the day, that there was no prophet seen in Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the friends of Judas came together, and said to Jonathan:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Since thy brother Judas died there is not a man like him to go forth against our enemies, Bacchides, and them that are the enemies of our nation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, therefore, we have chosen thee this day to be our prince, and captain, in his stead, to fight our battles.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Jonathan took upon him the government at that time, and rose up in the place of Judas, his brother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Bacchides had knowledge of it, and sought to kill him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan, and Simon, his brother, knew it, and all that were with them: and they fled into the desert of Thecua, and they pitched by the water of the lake Asphar,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Bacchides understood it, and he came himself, with all his army, over the Jordan, on the sabbath day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan sent his brother, a captain of the people, to desire the Nabutheans his friends, that they would lend them their equipage, which was copious.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Jambri came forth out of Madaba, and took John, and all that he had, and went away with them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After this it was told Jonathan, and Simon, his brother, that the children of Jambri made a great marriage, and were bringing the bride out of Madaba, the daughter of one of the great princes of Chanaan, with great pomp.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they remembered the blood of John, their brother: and they went up, and hid themselves under the covert of the mountain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they lifted up their eyes, and saw: and behold a tumult, and great preparation: and the bridegroom came forth, and his friends, and his brethren to meet them with timbrels, and musical instruments and many weapons.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they rose up against them from the place where they lay in ambush, and slew them, and there fell many wounded, and the rest fled into the mountains, and they took all their spoils:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the marriage was turned into mourning, and the noise of their musical instruments into lamentation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they took revenge for the blood of their brother: and they returned to the bank of the Jordan.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Bacchides heard it, and he came on the sabbath day even to the bank of the Jordan, with a great power.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan said to his company: Let us arise, and fight against our enemies: for it is not now as yesterday, and the day before.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For behold the battle is before us, and the water of the Jordan on this side and on that side, and banks, and marshes, and woods: and there is no place for us to turn aside.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, therefore, cry ye to heaven, that ye may be delivered from the hand of your enemies. And they joined battle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan stretched forth his hand to strike Bacchides, but he turned away from him backwards.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan, and they that were with him, leapt into the Jordan, and swam over the Jordan to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there fell of Bacchides’ side that day a thousand men: and they returned to Jerusalem,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they built strong cities in Judea, the fortress that was in Jericho, and in Ammaus, and in Bethoron, and in Bethel, and Thamnata, and Phara, and Thopo, with high walls, and gates, and bars.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he placed garrisons in them, that they might wage war against Israel:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he fortified the city of Bethsura, and Gazara, and the castle, and set garrisons in them, and provisions of victuals:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he took the sons of the chief men of the country for hostages, and put them in the castle in Jerusalem in custody.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now in the year one hundred and fifty-three, the second month, Alcimus commanded the walls of the inner court of the sanctuary to be thrown down, and the works of the prophets to be destroyed: and he began to destroy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At that time Alcimus was struck: and his works were hindered, and his mouth was stopped, and he was taken with a palsy, so that he could no more speak a word, nor give order concerning his house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Alcimus died at that time in great torment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Bacchides saw that Alcimus was dead: and he returned to the king, and the land was quiet for two years.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the wicked held a council, saying: Behold Jonathan, and they that are with him, dwell at ease and without fear: now, therefore, let us bring Bacchides hither, and he shall take them all in one night.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So they went, and gave him counsel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he arose to come with a great army: and he sent secretly letters to his adherents that were in Judea to seize upon Jonathan, and them that were with him: but they could not, for their design was known to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he apprehended of the men of the country, that were the principal authors of the mischief, fifty men, and he slew them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan, and Simon, and they that were with him, retired into Bethbessen, which is in the desert: and he repaired the breaches thereof, and they fortified it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Bacchides knew it, he gathered together all his multitude: and sent word to them that were of Judea.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he came, and camped above Bethbessen, and fought against it many days, and made engines.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Jonathan left his brother, Simon, in the city and went forth into the country, and came with a number of men,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And struck Odares, and his brethren, and the children of Phaseron, in their tents, and he began to slay, and to increase in forces.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Simon, and they that were with him, sallied out of the city, and burnt the engines,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they fought against Bacchides, and he was discomfited by them: and they afflicted him exceedingly, for his counsel, and his enterprise was in vain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he was angry with the wicked men that had given him counsel to come into their country, and he slew many of them: and he purposed to return with the rest into their country.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan had knowledge of it, and he sent ambassadors to him to make peace with him, and to restore to him the prisoners.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he accepted it willingly, and did according to his words, and swore that he would do him no harm all the days of his life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he restored to him the prisoners which he before had taken out of the land of Juda: and he returned, and went away into his own country, and he came no more into their borders.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the sword ceased from Israel: and Jonathan dwelt in Machmas, and Jonathan began there to judge the people, and he destroyed the wicked out of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now in the hundred and sixtieth year, Alexander, the son of Antiochus, surnamed the Illustrious, came up and took Ptolemais, and they received him, and he reigned there.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And king Demetrius heard of it, and gathered together an exceeding great army, and went forth against him to fight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Demetrius sent a letter to Jonathan, with peaceable words, to magnify him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he said: Let us first make a peace with him, before he make one with Alexander against us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he will remember all the evils that we have done against him, and against his brother, and against his nation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he gave him authority to gather together an army, and to make arms, and that he should be his confederate: and the hostages that were in the castle, he commanded to be delivered to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan came to Jerusalem, and read the letters in the hearing of all the people, and of them that were in the castle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they were struck with great fear, because they heard that the king had given him authority to gather together an army.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the hostages were delivered to Jonathan, and he restored them to their parents.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan dwelt in Jerusalem, and began to build, and to repair the city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he ordered workmen to build the walls, and mount Sion round about with square stones for fortification: and so they did.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the strangers that were in the strong holds, which Bacchides had built, fled away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And every man left his place, and departed into his own country:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Only in Bethsura there remained some of them, that had forsaken the law, and the commandments of God: for this was a place of refuge for them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And king Alexander heard of the promises that Demetrius had made Jonathan: and they told him of the battles, and the worthy acts that he and his brethren had done, and the labours that they had endured.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said: Shall we find such another man? now, therefore, we will make him our friend and our confederate.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So he wrote a letter, and sent it to him according to these words, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: King Alexander to his brother, Jonathan, greetings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We have heard of thee, that thou art a man of great power, and fit to be our friend:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now therefore, we make thee this day high priest of thy nation, and that thou be called the king’s friend, (and he sent him a purple robe, and a crown of gold) and that thou be of one mind with us in our affairs, and keep friendship with us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Jonathan put on the holy vestment in the seventh month, in the year one hundred and threescore, at the feast day of the tabernacles: and he gathered together an army, and made a great number of arms.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Demetrius heard these words, and was exceeding sorry, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What is this that we have done, that Alexander hath prevented us to gain the friendship of the Jews to strengthen himself?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I also will write to them words of request, and offer dignities, and gifts: that they may be with me to aid me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he wrote to them in these words: King Demetrius to the nation of the Jews, greeting.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whereas you have kept covenant with us, and have continued in our friendship, and have not joined with our enemies, we have heard of it, and are glad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore now continue still to keep fidelity towards us, and we will reward you with good things, for what you have done in our behalf.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And we will remit to you many charges, and will give you gifts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And now I free you, and all the Jews, from tributes, and I release you from the customs of salt, and remit the crowns, and the thirds of the seed:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the half of the fruit of trees, which is my share, I leave to you from this day forward, so that it shall not be taken of the land of Juda, and of the three cities that are added thereto out of Samaria and Galilee, from this day forth, and for ever:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And let Jerusalem be holy and free, with the borders thereof: and let the tenths, and tributes be for itself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I yield up also the power of the castle that is in Jerusalem, and I give it to the high priest, to place therein such men as he shall choose, to keep it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And every soul of the Jews that hath been carried captive from the land of Juda in all my kingdom, I set at liberty freely, that all be discharged from tributes, even of their cattle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I will that all the feasts, and the sabbaths, and the new moons, and the days appointed, and three days before the solemn day, and three days after the solemn day, be all days of immunity and freedom, for all the Jews that are in my kingdom:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And no man shall have power to do any thing against them, or to molest any of them, in any cause.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And let there be enrolled in the king’s army to the number of thirty thousand of the Jews: and allowance shall be made them, as is due to all the king’s forces and certain of them shall be appointed to be in the fortresses of the great king:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And some of them shall be set over the affairs of the kingdom, that are of trust, and let the governors be taken from among themselves, and let them walk in their own laws, as the king hath commanded in the land of Juda.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the three cities that are added to Judea, out of the country of Samaria, let them be accounted with Judea: that they may be under one, and obey no other authority but that of the high priest:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Ptolemais and the confines thereof, I give as a free gift to the holy places that are in Jerusalem, for the necessary charges of the holy things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I give every year fifteen thousand sickles of silver out of the king’s accounts, of what belongs to me:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all that is above, which they that were over the affairs the years before, had not paid, from this time they shall give it to the works of the house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover, the five thousand sickles of silver, which they received from the account of the holy places, every year, shall also belong to the priests that execute the ministry.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And whosoever shall flee into the temple that is in Jerusalem, and in all the borders thereof, being indebted to the king for any matter, let them be set at liberty, and all that they have in my kingdom, let them have it free.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the building also, or repairing the works of the holy places, the charges shall be given out of the king’s revenues:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the building also of the walls of Jerusalem, and the fortifying thereof round about, the charges shall be given out of the king’s account, as also for the building of the walls in Judea.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when Jonathan and the people heard these words, they gave no credit to them, nor received them because they remembered the great evil that he had done in Israel, for he had afflicted them exceedingly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And their inclinations were towards Alexander, because he had been the chief promoter of peace in their regard, and him they always helped.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And king Alexander gathered together a great army, and moved his camp near to Demetrius.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the two kings joined battle, and the army of Demetrius fled away, and Alexander pursued after him, and pressed them close.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the battle was hard fought, till the sun went down: and Demetrius was slain that day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Alexander sent ambassadors to Ptolemee king of Egypt, with words to this effect, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Forasmuch as I am returned into my kingdom and am set in the throne of my ancestors, and have gotten the dominion, and have overthrown Demetrius and possessed our country,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And have joined battle with him, and both he and his army have been destroyed by us, and we are placed in the throne of his kingdom:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, therefore, let us make friendship one with another: and give me now thy daughter to wife, and I will be thy son in law, and I will give both thee and her gifts worthy of thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And king Ptolemee answered, saying: Happy is the day wherein thou didst return to the land of thy fathers, and sattest in the throne of their kingdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And now I will do to thee as thou hast written but meet me at Ptolemais, that we may see one another, and I may give her to thee as thou hast said.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Ptolemee went out of Egypt, with Cleopatra his daughter, and he came to Ptolemais, in the hundred and sixty-second year.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And king Alexander met him, and he gave him his daughter, Cleopatra: and he celebrated her marriage at Ptolemais with great glory, after the manner of kings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And king Alexander wrote to Jonathan, that he should come and meet him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he went honourably to Ptolemais, and he met there the two kings, and he gave them much silver, and gold, and presents: and he found favour in their sight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And some pestilent men of Israel, men of a wicked life, assembled themselves against him, to accuse him: and the king gave no heed to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he commanded that Jonathan’s garments should be taken off, and that he should be clothed with purple: and they did so. And the king made him sit by himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to his princes: Go out with him into the midst of the city, and make proclamation, that no man complain against him of any matter, and that no man trouble him for any manner of cause.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So when his accusers saw his glory proclaimed, and him clothed with purple, they all fled away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king magnified him, and enrolled him amongst his chief friends, and made him governor, and partaker of his dominion.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan returned into Jerusalem with peace and joy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the year one hundred and sixty-five, Demetrius, the son of Demetrius, came from Crete into the land of his fathers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And king Alexander heard of it, and was much troubled, and returned to Antioch.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And king Demetrius made Apollonius his general, who was governor of Celesyria: and he gathered together a great army, and came to Jamnia: and he sent to Jonathan, the high priest,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Saying: Thou alone standest against us, and I am laughed at and reproached, because thou shewest thy power against us in the mountains.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, therefore, if thou trustest in thy forces, come down to us into the plain, and there let us try one another: for with me is the strength of war.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Ask, and learn who I am, and the rest that help me, who also say that your foot cannot stand before our face, for thy fathers have twice been put to flight in their own land:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And now how wilt thou be able to abide the horsemen, and so great an army in the plain, where there is no stone, nor rock, nor place to flee to?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when Jonathan heard the words of Apollonius, he was moved in his mind: and he chose ten thousand men, and went out of Jerusalem, and Simon, his brother, met him to help him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they pitched their tents near Joppe, but they shut him out of the city: because a garrison of Apollonius was in Joppe, and he laid siege to it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they that were in the city being affrighted, opened the gates to him: so Jonathan took Joppe.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Apollonius heard of it, and he took three thousand horsemen, and a great army.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he went to Azotus, as one that was making a journey, and immediately he went forth into the plain: because he had a great number of horsemen, and he trusted in them. And Jonathan followed after him to Azotus, and they joined battle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Apollonius left privately in the camp a thousand horsemen behind them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan knew that there was an ambush behind him, and they surrounded his army, and cast darts at the people from morning till evening.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the people stood still, as Jonathan had commanded them: and so their horses were fatigued.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Simon drew forth his army, and attacked the legion: for the horsemen were wearied: and they were discomfited by him, and fled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they that were scattered about the plain fled into Azotus, and went into Bethdagon, their idol’s temple, there to save themselves.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Jonathan set fire to Azotus, and the cities that were round about it, and took the spoils of them and the temple of Dagon: and all them that were fled into it, he burnt with fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So they that were slain by the sword, with them that were burnt, were almost eight thousand men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan, removed his army from thence and camped against Ascalon: and they went out of the city to meet him with great honour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan returned into Jerusalem with his people, having many spoils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it came to pass, when Alexander the king heard these words, that he honoured Jonathan yet more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he sent him a buckle of gold, as the custom is, to be given to such as are of the royal blood. And he gave him Accaron, and all the borders thereof, in possession.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king of Egypt gathered together an army, like the sand that lieth upon the sea shore, and many ships: and he sought to get the kingdom of Alexander by deceit, and join it to his own kingdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he went out into Syria with peaceable words and they opened to him the cities, and met him: for king Alexander had ordered them to go forth to meet him, because he was his father in law.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when Ptolemee entered into the cities, he put garrisons of soldiers in every city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he came near to Azotus, they shewed him the temple of Dagon that was burnt with fire, and Azotus, and the suburbs thereof, that were destroyed, and the bodies that were cast abroad, and the graves of them that were slain in the battle, which they had made near the way.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they told the king that Jonathan had done these things, to make him odious: but the king held his peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan came to meet the king at Joppe with glory, and they saluted one another, and they lodged there.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan went with the king as far as the river, called Eleutherus: and he returned into Jerusalem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And king Ptolemee got the dominion of the cities by the sea side, even to Seleucia, and he devised evil designs against Alexander.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he sent ambassadors to Demetrius, saying: Come, let us make a league between us, and I will give thee my daughter whom Alexander hath, and thou shalt reign in the kingdom of thy father.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For I repent that I have given him my daughter: for he hath sought to kill me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he slandered him, because he coveted his kingdom,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he took away his daughter, and gave her to Demetrius, and alienated himself from Alexander, and his enmities were made manifest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Ptolemee entered into Antioch, and set two crowns upon his head, that of Egypt, and that of Asia.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now king Alexander was in Cilicia at that time: because they that were in those places had rebelled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Alexander heard of it, he came to give him battle: and king Ptolemee brought forth his army, and met him with a strong power, and put him to flight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Alexander fled into Arabia, there to be protected: and king Ptolemee was exalted.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Zabdiel the Arabian took off Alexander’s head, and sent it to Ptolemee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And king Ptolemee died the third day after: and they that were in the strong holds were destroyed by them that were within the camp.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Demetrius reigned in the hundred and sixty-seventh year.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In those days Jonathan gathered together them that were in Judea, to take the castle that was in Jerusalem: and they made many engines of war against it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then some wicked men that hated their own nation, went away to king Demetrius, and told him that Jonathan was besieging the castle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he heard it, he was angry: and forthwith he came to Ptolemais, and wrote to Jonathan that he should not besiege the castle, but should come to him in haste, and speak to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when Jonathan heard this, he bade them besiege it still: and he chose some of the ancients of Israel, and of the priests, and put himself in danger.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he took gold, and silver, and raiment, and many other presents, and went to the king to Ptolemais and he found favour in his sight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And certain wicked men of his nation made complaints against him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king treated him as his predecessors had done before: and he exalted him in the sight of all his friends.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he confirmed him in the high priesthood and all the honours he had before, and he made him the chief of his friends.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan requested of the king that he would make Judea free from tribute, and the three governments, and Samaria, and the confines thereof: and he promised him three hundred talents.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king consented: and he wrote letters to Jonathan of all these things, to this effect.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: King Demetrius to his brother, Jonathan, and to the nation of the Jews, greeting.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We send you here a copy of the letter which we have written to Lasthenes, our parent, concerning you, that you might know it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: King Demetrius to Lasthenes, his parent, greetings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We have determined to do good to the nation of the Jews, who are our friends, and keep the things that are just with us, for their good will which they bear towards us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We have ratified, therefore, unto them all the borders of Judea, and the three cities, Apherema, Lydda, and Ramatha, which are added to Judea, out of Samaria, and all their confines, to be set apart to all them that sacrifice in Jerusalem, instead of the payments which the king received of them every year, and for the fruits of the land, and of the trees.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And as for other things that belonged to us of the tithes, and of the tributes, from this time we discharge them of them: the saltpans also, and the crowns that were presented to us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We give all to them, and nothing hereof shall be revoked from this time forth and for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, therefore, see that thou make a copy of these things, and let it be given to Jonathan, and set upon the holy mountain, in a conspicuous place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And king Demetrius, seeing that the land was quiet before him, and nothing resisted him, sent away all his forces, every man to his own place, except the foreign army, which he had drawn together from the islands of the nations: so all the troops of his fathers hated him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now there was one Tryphon who had been of Alexander’s party before: who seeing that all the army murmured against Demetrius, went to Emalchuel, the Arabian, who brought up Antiochus, the son of Alexander:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he pressed him much to deliver him to him, that he might be king in his father’s place: and he told him all that Demetrius had done, and how his soldiers hated him. And he remained there many days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan sent to king Demetrius, desiring that he would cast out them that were in the castle in Jerusalem, and those that were in the strong holds: because they fought against Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Demetrius sent to Jonathan, saying: I will not only do this for thee, and for thy people, but I will greatly honour thee, and thy nation, when opportunity shall serve.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, therefore, thou shalt do well if thou send me men to help me: for all my army is gone from me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan sent him three thousand valiant men to Antioch: and they came to the king, and the king was very glad of their coming.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they that were of the city assembled themselves together, to the number of a hundred and twenty thousand men, and would have killed the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king fled into the palace: and they of the city kept the passages of the city, and began to fight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king called the Jews to his assistance: and they came to him all at once, and they all dispersed themselves through the city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they slew in that day a hundred thousand men, and they set fire to the city, and got many spoils that day, and delivered the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they that were of the city saw that the Jews had got the city as they would: and they were discouraged in their mind, and cried to the king, making supplication, and saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Grant us peace, and let the Jews cease from assaulting us, and the city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they threw down their arms, and made peace, and the Jews were glorified in the sight of the king, and in the sight of all that were in his realm, and were renowned throughout the kingdom, and returned to Jerusalem with many spoils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So king Demetrius sat in the throne of his kingdom: and the land was quiet before him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he falsified all whatsoever he had said, and alienated himself from Jonathan, and did not reward him according to the benefits he had received from him, but gave him great trouble.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after this Tryphon returned, and with him Antiochus, the young boy, who was made king, and put on the diadem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there assembled unto him all the hands which Demetrius had sent away, and they fought against Demetrius who turned his back and fled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Tryphon took the elephants, and made himself master of Antioch.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And young Antiochus wrote to Jonathan, saying: I confirm thee in the high priesthood, and I appoint thee ruler over the four cities, and to be one of the king’s friends.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he sent him vessels of gold for his service, and he gave him leave to drink in gold, and to be clothed in purple, and to wear a golden buckle:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he made his brother, Simon, governor, from the borders of Tyre even to the confines of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Jonathan went forth, and passed through the cities beyond the river, and all the forces of Syria gathered themselves to him to help him, and he came to Ascalon, and they met him honourably out of the city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he went from thence to Gaza: and they that were in Gaza shut him out: and he besieged it, and burnt all the suburbs round about, and took the spoils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the men of Gaza made supplication to Jonathan, and he gave them the right hand: and he took their sons for hostages, and sent them to Jerusalem: and he went through the country, as far as Damascus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan heard that the generals of Demetrius were come treacherously to Cades, which is in Galilee, with a great army, purposing to remove him from the affairs of the kingdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he went against them: but left his brother, Simon, in the country.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Simon encamped against Bethsura, and assaulted it many days, and shut them up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they desired him to make peace, and he granted it them: and he cast them out from thence, and took the city, and placed a garrison in it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
